does: This paper systematically compares process supervision (feedback on intermediate reasoning steps) with outcome supervision (feedback on final answers) for training large language models to solve complex mathematical problems. The authors train models on the MATH dataset and demonstrate that process supervision substantially outperforms outcome supervision, achieving 78% accuracy on a representative test subset. The paper also investigates how active learning can further improve process supervision efficiency, and releases PRM800K, a large-scale dataset of step-level feedback annotations to facilitate future research on process-supervised learning.
connects: This work builds on recent interest in improving LLM reliability through different supervision paradigms, extending prior research comparing outcome versus process supervision. It responds to growing concerns about logical errors in multi-step reasoning by sophisticated models and connects to broader research on training methods for complex reasoning, including chain-of-thought prompting and intermediate step feedback. The paper engages with prior work on active learning for reducing annotation costs and continues investigations into which supervision signals most effectively guide models toward correct reasoning procedures rather than just correct answers.
enables: This research establishes process supervision as a more efficient training paradigm, opening pathways toward developing substantially more reliable reasoning systems through step-level feedback. The released PRM800K dataset enables future work on interpretability of reasoning processes, curriculum learning strategies, and improved verification methods for multi-step solutions. The findings motivate further investigation into combining process supervision with other techniques, scaling process supervision to broader domains beyond mathematics, and developing automated or semi-automated methods for generating step-level feedback at lower cost than human annotation.
